did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning for skin cancer staging identification. Specifically, it employed spectral preprocessing (SNV), dimensionality reduction (PLS), and classification models including Extreme Learning Machine (ELM), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Decision Tree, and Random Forest (RF). The Random Forest model achieved the highest staging accuracy (0.952 ± 0.014) for squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) using hyperspectral microscopic imaging data.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the collection of hyperspectral microscopic imaging (HMI) data cubes from skin tissue samples (BCC, MM, SCC). It details specific materials and methods, including the HMI system setup, spectral preprocessing (SNV, FD, SD, MSC), dimensionality reduction (PLS), and the development and validation of machine learning models (ELM, SVM, Decision Tree, RF) for SCC staging identification. The results section reports specific performance metrics (accuracy and kappa values) derived from these experiments, indicating it is an original research study rather than a review.

title: Staging of Skin Cancer Based on Hyperspectral Microscopic Imaging and Machine Learning
brief_summary: This study aimed to stage squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) using hyperspectral microscopic imaging (HMI) combined with machine learning. The researchers collected HMI data cubes from skin tissue samples including basal cell carcinoma, malignant melanoma, and SCC, specifically focusing on 63 SCC cases across stages I, II, and III. Spectral data were preprocessed using standard normal variate (SNV) transformation and reduced in dimensionality via partial least squares (PLS). Four classification models—Extreme Learning Machine, Support Vector Machine, Decision Tree, and Random Forest—were evaluated using a hold-out dataset partition. The Random Forest model achieved the highest staging accuracy of 0.952 ± 0.014 and a kappa value of 0.928 ± 0.022. The study concluded that spectral data from nuclear compartments contributed more significantly to accurate SCC staging than peripheral regions, demonstrating the potential of HMI and machine learning for non-invasive skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Included in the dataset collection (34 cases) alongside MM and SCC, but the primary experimental focus was on SCC staging. The paper states 'HMI data cubes of BCC, MM and SCC were collected' but the study 'studied the staging identification of SCC'.

Included in the dataset collection (39 cases) alongside BCC and SCC, but the primary experimental focus was on SCC staging. The paper states 'HMI data cubes of BCC, MM and SCC were collected' but the study 'studied the staging identification of SCC'.

The primary target of the study. 63 cases were used for staging identification (13 stage I, 37 stage II, 13 stage III). Additionally, 300 blind samples of SCC at stages I, II, and III were used for verification. The study focused on staging SCC using hyperspectral microscopic imaging and machine learning.

all_task_types.target: Squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) staging (Stage I, Stage II, Stage III)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification to identify the staging of squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) using hyperspectral microscopic imaging data. The study evaluates four classification models (Extreme Learning Machine, Support Vector Machine, Decision Tree, and Random Forest) to predict discrete diagnostic categories (stages I, II, and III). Performance was evaluated using accuracy and kappa values derived from confusion matrices.

datasets_used.name: Xi’an Alenabio and ZhongkeGuanghua Skin Tissue Samples
datasets_used.description: Hyperspectral microscopic imaging (HMI) data cubes collected from skin tissue samples purchased from Xi’an Alenabio (sample no: SK801c) and ZhongkeGuanghua (Xi’an) Intelligent Biotechnology (sample no: K683501). The dataset includes Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), and Malignant Melanoma (MM).

use.training: Used for SCC staging identification experiments. The 63 SCC cases were split into training and testing sets using a hold-out method with a 4:1 ratio. The SCC cases were further categorized by stage: 13 Stage I, 37 Stage II, and 13 Stage III.
use.validation: Not explicitly reported as a separate validation set; the hold-out method was used for partitioning.
use.testing: Used for testing the staging identification models (ELM, SVM, Decision Tree, RF) on the 20% hold-out subset of the 63 SCC cases. Also used for verification with 300 blind samples of SCC.
use.other: The 34 BCC and 39 MM cases were collected but the primary experimental focus was on SCC staging. 300 blind samples of SCC (stages I, II, III) were used for external verification of the optimal model.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Dataset partitioning into training and test sets with a 4:1 ratio.
Evaluation of model performance (number of correctly classified samples divided by total).
Testing the consistency of judgment, obtained based on the confusion matrix.
Reporting uncertainty/variability for accuracy and kappa values (e.g., 0.952 ± 0.014).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Model performance was evaluated using accuracy and kappa values derived from confusion matrices, with uncertainty reported as standard deviation.

SCC_Staging_Identification_Based_on_Spec.dataset_description: 63 SCC cases (13 Stage I, 37 Stage II, 13 Stage III) from Xi’an Alenabio and ZhongkeGuanghua. Split into training and test sets with a 4:1 ratio using the hold-out method.
SCC_Staging_Identification_Based_on_Spec.notes: SNV preprocessing performed best across all models. RF achieved the highest overall staging accuracy (0.952 ± 0.014) and kappa value (0.928 ± 0.022) when using SNV preprocessing.

SCC_Staging_Identification_Based_on_Diff.dataset_description: Subset of SCC data analyzed by selecting spectral regions from nuclear compartments vs. peripheral regions. Preprocessing: MSC and SNV. Models: ELM and RF.
SCC_Staging_Identification_Based_on_Diff.notes: Spectral data from nuclear compartments contributed more to accurate staging than peripheral regions. The highest accuracy reached 0.999 ± 0.001 (RF with SNV on nuclear compartments).

architecture_used.main_model: Four classification models were evaluated: Extreme Learning Machine (ELM), Support Vector Machine (SVM), Decision Tree, and Random Forest (RF). The Random Forest model achieved the best performance.
architecture_used.input_size: Spectral data from Hyperspectral Microscopic Imaging (HMI) with 151 bands (wavelength range 465.5–905.1 nm). Input dimensions were reduced to the first 10 principal components via Partial Least Squares (PLS).

ELM.weights: Input weights and hidden layer thresholds randomly generated; output weights uniquely determined.

Decision_Tree.type: Predictive model in tree diagram form (root, internal, leaf nodes)

RF.type: Ensemble learning classifier using multiple decision trees

training_details.preprocessing: Standard Normal Variate (SNV) performed best; other methods included FD, SD, MSC.
training_details.dimensionality_reduction: Partial Least Squares (PLS), selecting the first 10 principal components.

classification_or_output_head.task: Multi-class classification for SCC staging (Stage I, II, III)

preprocessing_techniques: Spectral preprocessing: Derivative method (First Derivative (FD), Second Derivative (SD)), Standard Normal Variate (SNV) transformation, Multiplicative Scatter Correction (MSC)
Dimensionality reduction: Partial Least Squares (PLS), selecting the first 10 principal components
Dataset partitioning: Hold-out method with a 4:1 ratio for training and testing sets
conclusions: The Random Forest (RF) model combined with Standard Normal Variate (SNV) preprocessing and Partial Least Squares (PLS) dimensionality reduction achieved the highest staging accuracy (0.952 ± 0.014) and kappa value (0.928 ± 0.022) for squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) staging identification.

Spectral data from nuclear compartments contributed significantly more to accurate SCC staging than spectral data from peripheral regions, with the RF model achieving an accuracy of 0.999 ± 0.001 when using nuclear compartment data.
The Standard Normal Variate (SNV) transformation was identified as the most effective spectral preprocessing method across all tested classification models (ELM, SVM, Decision Tree, and RF).

Partial Least Squares (PLS) was more effective than Principal Component Analysis (PCA) for extracting spectral features and compressing hyperspectral microscopic imaging (HMI) data, with the first 10 principal components selected for the final models.
Hyperspectral microscopic imaging combined with machine learning demonstrates potential as a non-invasive tool for skin cancer diagnosis and staging, offering high accuracy and consistency.
important_remarks: The study focuses exclusively on staging Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), while Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) and Malignant Melanoma (MM) were included in the dataset collection but not analyzed for staging.
The primary dataset is small (63 SCC cases) and sourced from only two commercial providers (Xi’an Alenabio and ZhongkeGuanghua), raising concerns about population and dataset bias.
The hold-out method (4:1 split) was used for model evaluation rather than cross-validation, which may lead to unstable performance estimates given the small sample size.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was reported; significance was inferred solely from accuracy and kappa metrics.

External validation was performed on 300 blind samples, but performance dropped significantly (accuracy 0.807 ± 0.025) compared to the internal test set (0.952 ± 0.014), indicating potential overfitting or domain shift.

The study demonstrates that spectral data from nuclear compartments yields significantly higher staging accuracy than peripheral regions, highlighting the critical importance of Region of Interest (ROI) selection.

The HMI system used is a specific push-broom setup with defined hardware parameters (151 bands, 465.5–905.1 nm), limiting generalizability to other hyperspectral imaging systems without re-calibration.
